content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

May 15, 2015

June Pujo
Planning Commissioner
City of Santa Barbara
City Hall
P.O. Box 1990
Santa Barbara, CA 93102-1990

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-15-089

Dear Ms. Pujo:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding your duties as a Santa Barbara
Planning Commissioner under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest or Section 1090.

FACTS

You are a City of Santa Barbara Planning Commissioner and in your private capacity a
professional land use consultant. Your spouse is an architect. Together, you and your spouse
operate as a 2-person S Corporation (P&A). You stated that a typical corporation would have a
substitute land use planner available to manage and present planning projects to the City in the case
where one of the corporation's staff is also a Planning Commissioner. In your case, however, you
do not have that option. You stated that you operate from a home office and while your husband is
qualified to submit and present architectural plans, he is not qualified to process complex planning
projects and does not have in-depth understanding of the zoning codes. You are concerned that this
results in a severe limitation on your ability to operate, accept work from clients in the city and to
provide good client services.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-15-089
Page No. 2

QUESTIONS AND ANALYSIS

Section 87100 provides:

"No public official at any level of state or local government shall make,
participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to
influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he
has a financial interest."

You have already acknowledged that you will not make or participate as a Planning
Commissioner in decisions affecting your business or your clients.² However, you are also
prohibited from influencing such a decision. A public official is attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or appears
before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency including any agency
appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of his or her agency. (Regulation 18704.3.)

1. Are you prohibited from presenting planning cases to City planners, to design review
boards and to staff hearing officer (zoning administrator) or City Council hearings?

The exception in Regulation 18704.4(b)(4) applies and allows you to prepare drawings or
submissions of an architectural, engineering or similar nature to be used by a client in connection
with a proceeding before any agency. However, this provision applies only if you have no other
direct oral or written contact with the agency with regard to the client's proceeding before the
agency except for necessary contact with agency staff concerning the processing or evaluation of
the drawings or submissions you prepared.

2. Are you prohibited from providing preliminary land use feasibility services to a client
within the city limits? By preliminary, you mean information and advice on city property prior to
formal permit application.

The Act does not prohibit your private conduct. However, if any decision before the
Planning Commission may financially affect a client, you may have to disqualify yourself from that
decision.

3. May you request information and/or interpretations from city planning/building/zoning
staff and may you attend pre-application meetings on a city project?

Contacts with your own agency are prohibited influencing if the contact is made for for the
purpose of influencing the decision. Communications to influence are still prohibited at any time or
place. However, merely requesting publically available information or attending a meeting would
not, in itself, be considered influencing a decision.

¹ When a public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200 (including Planning Commissioners)
has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she must: (1) immediately prior to the
discussion of the item, orally identify each type of economic interest involved in the decision as well as details of the
economic interest on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself, and (3) leave the room for the duration of
the discussion and/or vote on the item. (Section 87105; Regulation 18704.5.)

File No. A-15-089
Page No. 3


4. As a Planning Commissioner, is there a conflict of interest if your husband or you discuss specific client (or past client) information with other commissioners, council members, or other types of city committee members (e.g., design boards)? Does it make a difference whether or not the client relationship ended over a year ago, if the project was already permitted, or if the client relationship did not result in a formal permit application managed by P&A?

A public official must disqualify himself or herself from decisions affecting current clients of their business and past clients for 12 months after the last income was received. Assuming there is no future anticipated business with that client, you will not have a conflict of interest in discussing matters affecting that client. There may be other bodies of law that may limit discussion of former clients' private business, but we are not in a position to advise on such laws.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel,
Legal Division
